story:
“Hey.” I see him chalking in the street at the Midsummer Block Party. I slide into his space and take a piece of chalk because he doesn’t say no, which means we’re basically friends. I draw a jellyfish—bloopity bloop tentacles, powder on my fingers. He keeps drawing something I can’t quite tell—pirate? rain? roof people?

I try talking. “How old are you?” He says, “I’m six.” Me too —almost seven, really. I go on about birthdays and birthstones—mine’s July, ruby; his September, sapphire. I love gems. He finally looks me in the eyes like my mom says kids should. His eyes are brown (not tiger eye, just brown). Mine are blue (not sapphire-blue, just regular).

“Are you going to first grade?” Yes. Reading? Magic Tree House? I brag that I read a lot—maybe too much, because he looks back down. He says he doesn’t live here; his mom brought him and his little sister for “this thing,” and he wishes they hadn’t come. What’s his name? “Abel.” I hear “Apple,” but he says, “Abel.” I’m Jack—like the Jack in Magic Tree House—so that’s destiny. I tell him Midsummer is the best: bonfire, the maypole, staying up late, maybe being a NAKED JELLYFISH. He’s not laughing. His chalk snaps. He sighs.

“I saw something bad,” he says, “and I don’t know what to do.” I swear on my amethyst (amefist), my favorite, that I’ll keep his secret and help. “Is this about Alma swallowing the diamond ring while you were scared she’d choke on the cheetah rug?” I whisper. He tells me: after they got here, his little sister, Alma, needed the bathroom. Their mom let them use the lady’s house because she was busy helping with the party—“we kind of work for her,” he says. He washed his hands fast, wiped the sink to hide that they were there (boundaries), and when he came out, Alma was gone. He found her in the lady’s closet holding jewelry. He said her name too sharp, she startled, and she swallowed something. A ring. “Diamond,” Alma said. He panicked—afraid she’d choke and die on the cheetah rug—and now he’s terrified. Alma seems okay, but the ring is inside her, and if the lady finds out, she might call the police. His parents say they have to stay “under the radar.” He’s trapped between telling his mom (and maybe making everything worse) and not telling (which feels worse, because what if Alma isn’t okay?).

It feels exactly like a Jack-and-Annie adventure, except it’s real life and scary. I whisper that diamonds are super valuable. I want to keep my promise, but I know, for real life, swallowing a ring is serious. Secrets that can hurt someone aren’t secrets to keep. So I tell Abel I’ll help him find Alma right now—back at the lady’s house where he last saw her—and we’ll get a trusted grownup—my mom—so Alma can be safe first. If there’s a way to make it right with the lady, grownups will know how. No sneaking the ring back, no lying. Safety, then honesty.

We stand. Chalk dust on my swim trunks, on my knees. My happy-face jellyfish is unfinished; his drawing is a green blob now. We head to the lady’s porch, straight to the closet where he last saw her, past the shoes and the loud party voices, like a Where’s Waldo for a girl with a jewel belly. Our mission: find Alma, make sure she’s okay, and tell the truth in a way that keeps everyone safe. Then, maybe, we’ll still dance at the bonfire around the maypole—me, my old friends, my new friend Abel, and Alma, the girl who’d birth a stone. For real life.
